segments.s675-g41: سپاهست is the contracted poetic form of سپاه است (‘there is an army’); the copula is attached to the noun. با او ‘with him’ follows the verb phrase (typical Persian order can be inverted in poetry). فزون از شمار is a comparative phrase ‘more/greater than number’, where فزون (‘abundant, more’) is followed by از + شمار (‘than number’).
segments.s675-g42: A simple noun list: سواران (‘horsemen’) and گردان (‘troop, squadron’) joined by و. خنجرگزار is a compound noun modifying گردان (literally ‘dagger‑bearer’ or ‘those who carry daggers’). Word order is noun + modifier, as common in Persian compounds.
segments.s675-g43: اگر introduces a conditional clause. ما is the subject ‘we’. بگستهم is an archaic/poetic first‑person‑plural form built on گشودن/گستردن meaning ‘we spread/extend’; the prefixed بـ is the older present/subjunctive marker that appears frequently in classical Persian. یازیم (from یاخت/یاز) follows and means ‘we open/gape’, and دست (‘hand’) is the object/phrase being extended — literal ‘we spread and open (our) hand’. The order is inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s675-g44: بگیتی is a prepositional/time phrase ‘in the world’ or ‘among men’. نیابیم is the negative subjunctive/optative/indicative form (ن + یابیم) meaning ‘we do not find / we will not find’; in classical Persian the prefix ن negates the verb and the ب‑/ی‑ forms mark mood. جای نشست is a nominal phrase ‘a place to sit’ (جای = place; نشست used as a noun for ‘sitting/seat’).
segments.s675-g45: دگر here means ‘another’ or ‘one other’. آنک is a relative/demonstrative ‘that which/who’. باشد is the classical present/subjunctive copula ('is' / 'may be') used here to state existence/identity. دبیر کهن is a noun + adjective phrase ‘an old scribe/scholar’ with the adjective following the noun (typical Persian order).
segments.s675-g46: This is a relative clause modifying دبیر: که = ‘who/that’. برشاه is the prepositional phrase ‘to/upon the king’ (بـ + شاه). خواند is the classical poetic form of ‘read/recited’ (third person); in older Persian it appears without the short‑vowel endings of modern colloquial forms. گذشته سخن = ‘the past words/tale/poem’ — سخن here refers to a piece of speech or poem recited about past events.
segments.s675-g47: سواری (‘rider/horseman’) is the head noun. The relative clause که پرورده باشد is ‘who has been raised/trained’ (پرورده is a past participle; باشد is the classical copula). برزم is an archaic locative/adverbial (here ‘in war / for battle’), so the whole means ‘a rider who has been trained for the field/battle’. The word order places the relative clause after the noun, as usual.
segments.s675-g48: بداند is the older present form (بـ + داند) meaning ‘he/one knows’ (the b‑prefix marks classical present). همان نیز ‘the same also’ (همان = that/the same; نیز = also). آیین بزم is a noun phrase ‘the custom/practice (آیین) of the feast/banquet (بزم)’; together the line says that such a rider also knows the rites/manners of the feast. Word order: verb (بداند) can precede its object for emphasis in poetry.
segments.s675-g25: بر من فرستی is an address: بر + من (= 'to me') followed by فرستی ('you send' — 2nd person singular present). که begins a relative clause modifying the person to be sent; از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the battle' (از here = 'concerning/of', کارزار = 'battle').
segments.s675-g26: سخن گوید is the predicate of the relative clause: سخن ('speech, account') + گوید ('he/it speaks' — verb). و links a second coordinated clause. کرده باشد شکار is a perfective/periphrastic clause: کرده ('having done' / 'done') + باشد (auxiliary 'be' used here in optative/perfect sense) + شکار ('captured / prey'); together = 'and who has taken capture/prey.'
segments.s675-g27: دگر here means 'another' / 'one more'. آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'that / who' introducing a description; داننده is a participial noun 'knower, experienced one' modifying مرد ('man'), and کهن further qualifies him as 'old/ancient' — together 'another, that experienced old man.'
segments.s675-g28: که begins a relative clause referring back to the old man. از شهریاران is 'of/from the kings' (از = 'of/concerning' here), گزارد is the verb meaning roughly 'sets forth / utters / relates' and سخن is its object 'speech / tale' — 'who relates the speech/tales of the kings.'
segments.s675-g29: نوشته is a noun 'writing / written text' (a written work). یکی here means 'someone / a certain person' who is the agent; دفتر is the object 'book / volume'; آرد is the verb 'brings' (present tense), and مرا is the clitic object 'me' (marked here in two pieces in the segmentation). The whole line: 'Someone brings me a written book.'
segments.s675-g30: بدان is a compound/phrase meaning 'by/with that' (ب = 'to/with' + آن 'that'), introducing the consequence. درد و سختی = 'sorrow and hardship' (درد emotional sorrow here, سختی = 'hardship'). سرآرد is a verb 'to bring about / to befall' (here transitive: 'he brings upon me'); again مرا + را marks the object 'me'. So: 'by that he brings sorrow and hardship upon me.'
segments.s675-g31: آرزوی is 'desire / longing' and functions as the noun modified by the preceding 'سیم' (not separately listed): 'the silver (or radiance) of desire.' آنک is the relative 'that / which', خال is 'mole' (a small beauty-spot) and تواند is the modal verb 'can / is able' referring to what the mole can effect or be. The clause sketches what the mole (or that mark) can evoke.
segments.s675-g32: پرستنده is a noun/participle 'worshipper / admirer' (one who worships), و simply coordinates, ناهمال here is an adjective (given senses 'negligent / inattentive' in the list, though poetically it may mean 'without match / incomparable' in context), and تواند again is the potential 'can be.' Together: '(She) can be an object of worship and can be without equal.'
segments.s675-g1: چو at the start is the archaic/literary conjunction 'when'. The verbal phrase پنهان شد is a past/periphrastic passive ('became hidden' / 'was hidden') with شد as the main past verb. آن چادر آبنوس is a noun phrase with the demonstrative آن before the head noun چادر and the adjective/qualifier آبنوس after it (typical classical word order).
segments.s675-g2: بگوش آمد is an idiomatic verbal cluster: بگوش ('to the ear', i.e. 'to be heard') + آمد ('came' = 'was heard'). از marks source/agent 'from/away from', دوربانگ is a compound noun meaning a distant cry/echo, and خروش is the sound 'roar/cry' that is heard; the phrase orders 'source/qualifier' after آمد, as in poetic diction.
segments.s675-g3: جهانگیر is a personal/name noun placed as the grammatical subject. شد is the past-tense verb 'became/came' (here marking the action 'came up'). تابنزد is a fused literary prepositional expression meaning 'as far as, up to (the) near (of)'; پدر is the object of that spatial relation ('to/near the father'). The clause thus expresses movement/approach toward the father.
segments.s675-g4: نهانش is an adverbial/qualifying form meaning 'inwardly/secretly' (نهان + enclitic -ش 'his'), followed by پر + از indicating 'full of' (پر از درد = 'full of pain'). وخسته links with جگر: وخسته جگر is a poetic nominal phrase 'and [with] weary heart' where جگر functions as the idiomatic 'heart/seat of feeling'. The clause is elliptical (no copula) and describes internal state.
segments.s675-g5: چو again is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دیدش is the past verb دید + enclitic object -ش 'him' ('he saw him'). بنالید is a past intransitive verb 'moaned/lamented'. و simply coordinates the second verb. بردش here is the past of بردن with enclitic -ش 'him' and means 'carried/took him' (the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense, so the appropriate sense index is not available). نماز is the object/noun 'prayer', so the cluster likely means he took/carried him for/into prayer or performed a prayer over him in this context.
segments.s675-g6: همی‌بود is the literary imperfect/continuous past 'continued to be / was' (here functioning as 'remained'). پیشش is a locative/relational prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + enclitic -ش). زمانی دراز is a temporal noun phrase 'a long while' (زمانی = 'a while/for a time', دراز = 'long'), completing the clause 'he remained before him for a long time.'
segments.s675-g7: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial 'to him' (به + او contracted in classical style). گفت here is the speaker's verb 'said', but the supplied sense list does not include the simple past 'said', so no direct sense index fits. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (literary), شاه is the vocative 'king', and نابختیار is an epithet 'ill‑fated/unlucky' addressing him.
segments.s675-g8: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here introducing source/association). نوشین qualifies روان, yielding 'the sweet/delightful soul' (nominative/genitive relation). در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world', and یادگار is the noun 'remembrance/memento' — the sequence forms an appositional/genitival description: 'a remembrance in the world of the sweet soul.'
segments.s675-g57: پسر (subject) + مهربان‌تر (comparative adjective formed with -تر) + بد + از شهریار is a compact copular comparison: بد here stands for the poetic/past copula (classical contraction of بود), so the clause reads 'The son was kinder than the king.' The preposition از introduces the standard of comparison ('than the king').
segments.s675-g58: بدین = به این ('to/with this') is a poetic fused form introducing the following action; داستان زد uses زد (literally 'struck') in its literary sense 'told/uttered' (common in classical Persian to mean 'recounted'); یکی هوشیار is the agent 'a certain sensible/alert man'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'To this tale (or at this) a sensible man spoke/told.'
segments.s675-g59: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding 'one' (یکی هوشیار). یار here means 'companion/friend' or an epithet (a man), and زبان ... سخن is a poetic collocation: زبان (tongue/speech) qualified by چرب و شیرین (flattering/pleasant and sweet). The adjectives follow the noun phrase in a compact, figurative description: 'a companion whose tongue was flattering and sweet in speech.'
segments.s675-g60: که continues the same relative clause. از marks source or cause; پیر نستوه is a nominal phrase 'an old, distressed/wearied (man)' where نستوه is an adjectival qualifier after پیر. گشته is the perfect/participial form of شدن 'has become', and کهن is a predicate adjective 'old/ancient.' Together: 'who, worn/distressed by age, had become (very) old.'
segments.s675-g61: هنرمند‌گر here functions as a nominal/conditional phrase: هنرمند 'craftsman/artist' with گر (classical/poetic 'if' or conditional marker) implied; the following مردم بی‌هنر names the environment 'people who are unskilled.' The clause sets a condition ('If a skilled man is among unskilled people...'), with the verb omitted (a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s675-g62: بفرجام is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in the end/at last.' هم = 'even/also.' خاک here = 'earth/dust' used figuratively for burial or being reduced to dust. دارد + ببر forms a verbal idea: دارد 'has' plus (an infinitive/old verbal form) ببر 'to take' — idiomatically 'has the earth to take [him]' = 'the earth ultimately claims him.' This is a compact poetic way to say even such a person is finally reduced to dust.
segments.s675-g17: 'بدو گفت هرمزد‌ای پرخرد' is an address embedded with the reporting verb. 'بدو' is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; 'گفت' is the past tense verb 'said'; 'هرمزد‌ای پرخرد' is a vocative ('هرمزد' + vocative particle 'ای') with the adjective 'پرخرد' 'very wise' following the name. The whole segment reads 'he said to him: O wise Ahura Mazda'.
segments.s675-g18: This clause is a wish/optative: 'همین روز سختی' = 'this very day of hardship' (determiner 'همین' + noun phrase), followed by 'ز من بگذرد' where 'ز' = 'from', 'من' = 'me', and 'بگذرد' is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive (optative) of 'گذراندن/گَذَشتن' meaning 'may it pass' or 'let it pass (from me)'. Word order places the noun phrase first and the verb (optative) at the end.
segments.s675-g19: 'مرا نزد تو آرزو بد سه چیز' literally orders the constituents unusually: 'مرا' (object form 'me') + 'نَزد تو' ('in/at your presence') sets the scene, then 'آرزو' = 'wish(s)'. The word 'بد' here is an archaic/poetic imperative/verb-form meaning 'give/grant' (classical contraction of 'بده'), so the sequence means 'grant me three wishes in your presence' or 'I ask of you, before you, three wishes.'
segments.s675-g20: 'برین بر فزونی نخواهیم نیز' qualifies the three requests: 'برین' = 'on/for these' (an older form = 'these'), 'بر فزونی' = 'any further increase/beyond (them)', and 'نخواهیم' is the 1st-person plural negative of 'خواستن' meaning 'we will not want/seek'. 'نیز' adds 'also/indeed' for emphasis. Together: 'of these (three) we will not seek more.'
segments.s675-g21: 'یکی آنک شبگیر هر بامداد' begins a numbered wish: 'یکی' = 'one'/'a certain (one)', 'آنک' functions as a relative/introducer 'who/which', 'شبگیر' = 'night-visitor' (one who comes by night), and 'هر بامداد' = 'each/ every morning (at dawn)'. The phrase sets up 'one who, coming at night, at every dawn ...'.
segments.s675-g22: The verb clause 'کنی گوش ما را به آواز شاد' attaches to the relative: 'کنی' is the present/subjunctive form of 'کردن' used here as 'cause / make' (classical/poetic subjunctive); 'گوش ما را' is the definite object 'our ear(s)' marked by 'را'; 'به آواز شاد' is the goal/means 'with/to a joyful song'. Structure: 'may he cause our ears to [receive/hear] a joyful song' = 'make our ears glad with a joyful song.'
segments.s675-g23: 'و دیگر سواری ز گردنکشان' gives the second wish: 'و' = 'and', 'دیگر' = 'another', 'سواری' = 'a rider/horseman', 'ز' = 'from/among', and 'گردنکشان' = 'the rebels' (literally 'those who lift their necks', i.e. proud/rebellious ones). So: 'and another, a rider from among the rebels.'
segments.s675-g24: 'که از رزم دیرینه دارد نشان' is a relative clause describing that rider: 'که' = 'who/that', 'از' = 'from/of', 'رزم دیرینه' = 'ancient / long‑standing warfare/battles', 'دارد' = 'has/possesses', and 'نشان' = 'a sign/mark/trace'. Word order places the possessed noun 'نشان' at the end: 'who bears the marks of old battles.'
segments.s675-g9: تو دانی = present tense statement 'you know' (تو = vocative/old poetic 'thou'); که‌گر = conditional 'if' introducing the subordinate clause; بودمی is an archaic past/subjunctive form meaning 'I were / I had been'. پشت تو is a possessive phrase where پشت ('back', here 'support/protection') precedes the pronoun تو (your). The clause therefore means 'you know that if I had been your support...'.
segments.s675-g10: بسوزن is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'would burn' / 'would be burning' (optative/conditional sense here). نخستی is an ordinal/adjective 'the first' modifying the following noun phrase; سر انگشت is a compound meaning 'the tip/head of the finger' ('سر' = head/tip + انگشت = finger). تو is the possessive pronoun 'your' attached after the noun phrase. The whole segment expresses that the very first fingertip of yours would burn.
segments.s675-g11: نگر is an imperative 'look' or 'see'. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'تا چه فرمایی' meaning 'to see what you will say/command'; چه = 'what'. فرمایی is a present/subjunctive/polite form 'you will say' or 'you may say' (here 'you will say'). اکنون is the adverb 'now'. مرا (written here split as م + را) is the object 'me' with the object marker را attached; word order places the time adverb near the verb phrase.
segments.s675-g12: غم آمد is a simple past clause 'sorrow came' (i.e., 'I became sorrowful' or 'sorrow befell me'); the verb آمد is past 'came'. تو را topicalizes/marks 'you' as the affected party (تو + را). دل پر از خون is a noun phrase 'a heart full of blood' (دل = heart, پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of/because of', خون = blood). مرا (again split as م + را) indicates 'because of me' / 'toward me' in the context; the sequence means 'sorrow has come to me; for you, [your] heart is full of blood because of me.'
segments.s675-g13: گر‌ای دون combines the conditional particle گر ('if') with the vocative ای + دون ('O lowly/mean one') addressing the addressee. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/who' or simply links the conditional content. فرمان دهی is present/subjunctive 'you give an order' (فرمان = command, دهی = 'you give'). بر درت is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at your door/gate' (بر = on/at, درت = 'your door/gate' with enclitic).
segments.s675-g14: یکی in this context means 'one' or 'a certain' (introducing a single person). بنده‌ام is 'I am a servant' or 'I have a servant' depending on reading; here it functions as 'I (or I have) a servant' with the enclitic -ام. پاسبان سرت is a noun phrase 'guardian of your head' (پاسبان = guard, سرت = 'your head' / 'for you' with possessive suffix). Grammatically the phrase says there is one servant (or I have one servant) who acts as the guard of your head.
segments.s675-g15: نجویم is the negative of جویم: here 'I do not seek' / 'I will not seek' (ن- negation + جویم). کلاه in poetic usage often means 'trick' or 'deceit' (literally 'hat'), the object of نجویم. و is the conjunction 'and / nor'. نخواهم is the negative future/volitional 'I will not (do)', and سپاه is its object 'army' — the clause implies 'I will not bring/command an army'.
segments.s675-g16: ببرم is a present/subjunctive form 'I will take / I will carry' (بردن). سرخویش is a fused expression meaning 'one's own head / one's person' (here 'my head' or 'my own person'); the exact lexical analysis is archaic/poetic (the provided lemma senses do not match this occurrence). در پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of the king' (در = into/in, پیش = before/in the presence of, شاه = king). The segment states the speaker will personally bring his head before the king (i.e., submit himself personally).
segments.s675-g49: ازین = از + این (literally “from this / from now”); هر زمان نو is an adverbial phrase “every new time.” فرستم is first person singular present/future (poetic) “I send / I will send.” یکی is the indefinite object “one / someone,” placed after the verb (postverbal object order common in Persian poetry). Together: “From now, at every new time I will send one.”
segments.s675-g50: تو = vocative/subject “you.” با introduces cause/association “with / because of.” درد here denotes emotional suffering (“sorrow/pain”). پژمان is an adjective/noun meaning “remorseful / regret.” مباش is the negative imperative (2nd person) “do not be.” اندکی is a degree adverb “a little / even a bit,” modifying the prohibition (i.e. “do not be even a little remorseful”). The whole clause: “Do not be (even a little) remorseful because of the pain.”
segments.s675-g51: مدان is an archaic/formal verbal imperative-like form (here used negatively: 'do not think / do not take'); این = “this.” زگستهم is an archaic compound/adverbial (uncertain in modern Persian) that links or colours the clause (hence translated loosely as “so / thus”); کاین is the complementizer k- + این, introducing the content clause “that this …”; ایزدیست = ایزدی + است, predicate “is divine / godly.” Grammatically this is a negative/imperative admonition followed by a complement clause: “Do not take this so — that this is divine.”
segments.s675-g52: ز is the poetic preposition/particle meaning “from / because of,” introducing the ground or cause. گفتار و کردار lists the grounds: “words/speech and deeds/actions.” نابخردیست is نابخردی + است, a nominal predicate meaning “is folly / is foolishness.” The structure gives a causal/explanatory remark: “for/from its speech and action it is foolish.”
segments.s675-g53: دل تو = “your heart” (possessive phrase; possessor follows head in poetry). بدین here corresponds to به + این (“with/in this”); درد = “pain” (the object/ground of the sentiment). خرسند is an adjective “pleased, content”; باد is the optative particle meaning “may [it] be” (3rd person optative). The clause is a blessing/wish: “May your heart be content with this pain.”
segments.s675-g54: همان = “that same / the same (aforementioned).” با = “with.” خرد = “wisdom” (here the desirable companion). نیز = “also / likewise.” پیوند = “bond / linkage.” باد is again the optative “may it be.” The word order is poetic: “May that same, also, be joined with wisdom.”
segments.s675-g55: بگفت is past tense “he said / he told.” این is the direct object “this.” و = “and.” گریان is a participial/adjectival form “weeping” functioning adverbially (describing his manner). بیامد is past “he came” (narrative past). زپیش is a poetic adverb “from before / forward / toward the front” (i.e. he approached). Word order: verb + object, conjunction, manner participle + verb + adverbial.
segments.s675-g56: نکرد is the negative past of کردن “did not do / did not make.” آشکارا is an adverb “openly / manifestly” modifying the negative verb. بکس in the manuscript is orthographically fused (بِ کس = “to any person / to anyone”); here it functions as the indirect object “to anyone” (note: the provided lemma senses are not a perfect match; this is a fused prepositional phrase in classical spelling). راز خویش = “his secret” (راز = secret; خویش is the reflexive/possessive element ‘his/own’; the final ش is the possessive suffix). Together: “He did not openly make his secret known to anyone.”
segments.s675-g33: نبینند is a negative optative/jussive 3rd‑person plural form meaning 'may they not see' (literary/poetic wish). زین پس is an archaic adverbial phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (zīn here = archaic 'this/now' + پس = 'after'), followed by the object جهان را with the postposed object marker را. بچشم is a prefixed construction (بـ + چش(م)) used here as an instrumental/optative notion 'with the eye; to behold / experience' linking to نبینند.
segments.s675-g34: This clause continues the wish: بریشان here functions as بر + (ِ) ایشان 'upon them / to them' (archaic fused form). برانی is a verbal/optative sense 'may it drive away / banish' (poetic causative/jussive idea). برین is the demonstrative 'this' (بر + ین, an archaic spelling of این) modifying سوک 'dirge/mourning', and خشم is the noun 'anger' in a genitive/qualifying link 'the dirge of anger'.
segments.s675-g35: بدو گفت خسرو is an inverted word order 'to him said Khosrow' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' as a datival pronoun + گفت 'said' with خسرو as subject). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative: که‌ای (poetic/colloquial vocative particle + ای) addresses the addressee as 'O...', followed by شهریار 'sovereign/king'.
segments.s675-g36: مباد is a fixed negative optative formula 'may it not be'. آنک introduces the subordinate clause 'that / which'; برچشم تو is an idiomatic locution meaning 'in/for your eye' (بِـ + چشم = 'in the sight of'), i.e. 'in your view'; سوکوار is a noun 'archer/marksman' used as the predicate of the subordinate clause.
segments.s675-g37: نباشد is a negative optative/jussive 'may it not be' (formulaic wish). و گرچه بود is a concessive clause 'and although it was / even if it were' (گرچه = 'although/even if', بود = past/optative copula here marking the hypothetical). درنهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' (archaic fused form of در نهان).
segments.s675-g38: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بدخواه تو is a genitival phrase 'your ill‑wisher / enemy' (بدخواه = 'one who wishes you ill', تو = 'your'). دور is an adjective/adverb 'far, distant'. بادازجهان is a fixed optative/benedictive formula combining باد (optative 'may [he] be') + از جهان 'from the world' — 'may he be blown/removed from the world', i.e. 'may he be far from the world' or 'may he perish.'
segments.s675-g39: ولیکن is a conjunctive 'but / however'. نگه کن is an imperative 'look! / pay attention!' formed from نگه (gaze, look) + the auxiliary/imperative کن. بروشن روان is an adjectival phrase 'bright/clear of spirit' (بـروشن = 'bright/clear', روان = 'soul/spirit'), describing the subject to be noticed.
segments.s675-g40: که here functions as a causal/subordinating 'for / because'. بهرام is the proper noun subject. چوبینه is instrumental/qualifying — 'club‑made' or 'club‑wielding' (literally 'wooden/with a club') modifying the predicate. شد پهلوان is a past tense copular construction 'became a champion' (شد = 'became', پهلوان = 'champion, hero').

sentences.s675-s21: With him is an army greater than number — horsemen and squadrons of dagger‑bearers.
sentences.s675-s22: If we spread and open our hand, in the world we shall not find a place to sit.
sentences.s675-s23: Another (one) is that old scribe who recited to the king the speech/words of the past.
sentences.s675-s24: A rider who has been trained for battle knows that same custom of the feast as well.
sentences.s675-s13: Send (to) me someone who will speak of the battle and who has made a capture (or taken prey).
sentences.s675-s14: Another — that knowing, aged man — who relates the tales of the kings.
sentences.s675-s15: Someone brings me a written volume; by that (he) brings upon me sorrow and hardship.
sentences.s675-s16: The silver (brightness) of desire that a mole might have — (she) can be adored and can be without equal.
sentences.s675-s1: When that ebony veil was hidden, a roar was heard from afar.
sentences.s675-s2: Jahangir came up to his father; inwardly he was full of pain and his heart was weary.
sentences.s675-s3: When he saw him he moaned and carried him; he stayed before him for a long time.
sentences.s675-s4: He said to him, 'O ill‑fated king, you are a remembrance in the world of that sweet soul.'
sentences.s675-s29: The son was kinder than the king; to this tale a sensible man spoke.
sentences.s675-s30: who was a companion with a smooth, sweet tongue in speech, and who, worn and distressed with age, had grown old.
sentences.s675-s31: If a craftsman is among unskilled people, in the end even the soil claims him (i.e., he is reduced to dust).
sentences.s675-s9: He said to him, “O wise Ahura Mazda, may this very hard day pass from me.”
sentences.s675-s10: I ask, in your presence, three wishes; beyond these we will desire no more.
sentences.s675-s11: One: that a night-visitor, every dawn, make our ears glad with a joyful song.
sentences.s675-s12: And another: a horseman from the rebels who bears the marks of long‑standing battles.
sentences.s675-s5: You know that if I had been at your back (your protector), the very first tip of your finger would have burned.
sentences.s675-s6: Look, to see what you will say; now sorrow has come to me — your heart is full of blood because of me.
sentences.s675-s7: If, O lowly one, you give a command at your gate, I have one servant who is the guardian of your head.
sentences.s675-s8: I will not seek tricks, nor will I want an army; I will carry my own head into the presence of the king.
sentences.s675-s25: From now on I will send one at each new time; do not be overcome with sorrow because of the pain — not even a little.
sentences.s675-s26: Do not think this so — that this is divine; from its words and deeds it is folly.
sentences.s675-s27: May your heart be content with this pain; may that same (contentment) also be joined with wisdom.
sentences.s675-s28: He said this and came forward weeping; he did not openly make his secret known to anyone.
sentences.s675-s17: May they never again behold the world with their eyes; may this dirge of anger drive them away.
sentences.s675-s18: Khosrow said to him, 'O sovereign, may it not be that he is an archer in your sight.'
sentences.s675-s19: May it not be so; and though it be true in secret, may your ill‑wisher be blown away from the world.
sentences.s675-s20: But look — observe the bright spirit; for Bahram has become a club‑wielding champion.
